patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 14-year-old anxious 8th-grade student. You speak vaguely unless asked follow-ups, and you worry about costs and missing school/your parent missing work.

Start by saying your wrists and ankles have been getting weaker and more painful, you keep spraining your ankles easily, and your hands/feet look “misshapen,” especially wrists and tops of feet. Volunteer difficulty gripping/opening jars and doing push-ups.

If asked for details, say it’s been slowly worsening for a few years, with brief morning stiffness (not hours), on-and-off swelling after use, weaker fingers without obvious swelling, shoes fitting differently because feet are flatter/wider, and you’re more tired.

Do NOT mention foamy urine, puffiness around eyes/ankles, peeing less, or high BP unless specifically asked about urine, swelling, blood pressure, or kidney issues.

If asked about recent illness, mention a short fever/blotchy rash after mosquito bites with red/irritated eyes and body aches; worry it could be Zika/infection.

Resist admitting you’re avoiding social events and taking lots of ibuprofen unless asked directly; then admit, embarrassed and afraid of judgment.

If the clinician is empathetic, you soften, thank them, and become more specific.

diagnosis.primary: multicentric carpo-tarsal osteolysis with or without nephropathy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Multicentric carpotarsal osteolysis (MCTO) with or without nephropathy
required_evidence: Progressive osteolysis predominantly affecting carpal and/or tarsal bones with pain, swelling, deformity, and functional limitation
Noninflammatory or minimally inflammatory systemic labs (often normal/low ESR and CRP despite symptoms)
Exclusion of infectious arthritis/osteomyelitis and inflammatory arthritides as primary causes
Assessment for nephropathy: urinalysis for protein/hematuria and renal function testing (creatinine/eGFR) given known association
Family history or genetic confirmation (MAFB variant) if available/appropriate

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia of chronic inflammation expected early)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (no leukocytosis expected in noninfectious osteolysis)
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal to mildly elevated; often normal
expected_lab_results.CRP: Normal to mildly elevated; often normal
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Potassium: Typically normal unless renal involvement; may be elevated with advanced nephropathy

medications: Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 60 mg/kg/day; do not exceed 3,000 mg/day in adolescents/adults unless specifically directed)
Topical diclofenac 1% gel apply 2 g to affected hand/foot joints up to 4 times daily as needed (avoid if history of NSAID hypersensitivity extends beyond ibuprofen)
non_pharmacological: Refer to pediatric rheumatology/orthopedics for confirmation, imaging review, and longitudinal management
Screen for nephropathy: urinalysis (protein/hematuria), urine protein/creatinine ratio, serum creatinine/eGFR; refer to nephrology if abnormal
Immobilization/splinting during painful flares; occupational/physical therapy for function, joint protection, and adaptive devices
Avoid high-impact activities that worsen pain; activity modification with graded strengthening as tolerated
Genetic counseling and consideration of MAFB testing (patient and family) when appropriate
Consider bone health optimization (ensure adequate calcium/vitamin D intake; assess vitamin D level if clinically indicated)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms and function in 2-4 weeks; earlier (within 1 week) if worsening pain, fever, new swelling/redness, or any abnormal renal screening results

must_include: Condition involves progressive breakdown (osteolysis) of specific wrist/hand and ankle/foot bones
Symptoms can mimic arthritis but inflammation/infection markers are often not strongly elevated
Some patients develop kidney involvement; screening is important even if no urinary symptoms
Goals: pain control, preserve function, prevent deformity/complications, and monitor kidneys
Analgesia plan prioritizes non-NSAID options due to ibuprofen allergy; avoid ibuprofen and review safety of any NSAID use
Need for specialist follow-up (rheumatology/orthopedics; nephrology if renal findings)
Discuss uncertainty and need to rule out alternative causes (infection/inflammatory arthritis) if red flags appear
Provide return precautions: fever, rapidly worsening swelling/redness, inability to bear weight/use hand, new rash, decreased urination, swelling around eyes/legs
Discuss potential genetic nature and implications for family members
Joint protection strategies and activity modification to reduce stress on wrists/ankles/feet
Importance of adherence to therapy/splinting and follow-up appointments
Avoid self-treatment with ibuprofen or related NSAIDs without clinician approval due to allergy

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms (wrist/hand and ankle/foot pain, swelling, stiffness), functional limitations, onset/progression, trauma, systemic symptoms, red flags for infection/inflammatory arthritis, and family history/genetic context.
Assess kidney involvement symptoms and risks (hematuria/foamy urine, edema, decreased urine output), prior renal disease, and current medications/supplements.

Order screening labs to evaluate nephropathy and alternative etiologies: urinalysis (protein/hematuria), urine protein-to-creatinine ratio, serum creatinine/eGFR, electrolytes; inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) and CBC as needed to assess infection/inflammation.
Review labs to support MCTO suspicion, identify renal involvement, and determine urgency of nephrology referral or additional workup.
Synthesize history and results to diagnose multicentric carpo-tarsal osteolysis with or without nephropathy; document differential and rationale (mimics inflammatory arthritis/infection).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (osteolysis pattern, arthritis mimic, low markers), explain treatment goals and monitoring, address concerns/return precautions/genetic implications, and lifestyle/joint protection guidance.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm ibuprofen allergy details (reaction type, severity, timing) and assess cross-reactivity risk with other NSAIDs.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: review medication list for interactions with proposed analgesics (e.g., acetaminophen combinations, opioids, adjuvants) and any renal-risk medications.

Provide a pain control plan prioritizing non-NSAID options due to ibuprofen allergy; avoid ibuprofen and avoid/limit NSAIDs pending allergy type and renal status; include dosing guidance and safety counseling.

Arrange specialist follow-up: rheumatology/orthopedics for function/deformity prevention; nephrology if renal abnormalities; plan monitoring intervals and repeat renal labs/urine studies as appropriate.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, documenting return precautions, and ensuring follow-up plan.

general_concerns: Believes hereditary spastic paraplegia is basically the same as ALS and expects rapid paralysis within a year or two.
Thinks the absent Achilles reflex means his nerves are 'dying' quickly and that walking will permanently worsen the damage.
Assumes a normal brain/spine MRI would rule out hereditary spastic paraplegia, so he doubts the diagnosis if imaging is unremarkable.
Believes genetic conditions always affect children, so he thinks having no kids yet means he can ignore family planning discussions.
Thinks there must be a vitamin or supplement that can 'reverse' the condition if he finds the right one online.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male IT support specialist. You are anxious but communicate clearly and logically. Start by saying: “My legs keep getting stiffer and I’m tripping more when I walk.” Early in the visit, volunteer: legs feel tight/stiff (worse walking fast/uneven ground), toe-tripping, stairs harder needing handrail, legs fatigue quickly and walking distance reduced.

If asked, add: nighttime leg spasms/jerks, feet point down/in with uneven shoe wear, you were told your Achilles reflex is missing, near-falls and one fall, urinary urgency.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: back/hip soreness, furniture-walking in dim light, worse balance when tired/stressed, hand clumsiness, weak posture/neck/trunk.

Resist/avoid at first: urinary accidents, sexual difficulties, feeling down/social avoidance; admit only with gentle, direct questions.

Express misconceptions: think it’s a pinched back nerve; worry about MS; occasional tingling makes you fear neuropathy/poor circulation.

Raise cost/job fears about PT, braces/canes, and workplace disclosure. If clinician shows empathy, you become more open and ask about affordable options and work accommodations.

confounders: Borderline low vitamin B12 level (can mimic myelopathy/neuropathy)

Mild CK elevation from chronic spasticity and reduced mobility (may suggest myopathy)
History of intermittent heavy alcohol use (can contribute to neuropathy and gait instability)
Remote lumbar disc disease with episodic radicular pain (can complicate lower extremity reflex interpretation, including absent Achilles reflex)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary spastic paraplegia (HSP), uncomplicated/pure type
acceptable_alternatives: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) with predominant UMN signs
required_evidence: Progressive lower-extremity spasticity and stiffness with upper motor neuron pattern (spastic gait, hyperreflexia at knees, increased tone)
Chronic course with minimal/no sensory level and no prominent bowel/bladder involvement in pure HSP
Family history consistent with inherited pattern OR genetic confirmation when available
Absent Achilles reflex can be present if there is concomitant peripheral neuropathy; document otherwise preserved strength and lack of lower motor neuron findings
No red flags for acute cord compression (no acute onset, severe back pain, rapid progression) and no systemic infection/malignancy signs
Exclusion of structural spinal cord lesion and treatable mimics (e.g., B12 deficiency) based on history/exam and appropriate testing/imaging when indicated

expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal baseline (needed prior to certain meds)

medications: Tizanidine 2 mg PO at bedtime; increase by 2 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated to 2-4 mg PO three times daily (max 36 mg/day)
Gabapentin 100-300 mg PO at bedtime; titrate every 3-7 days to 300 mg PO three times daily as tolerated for spasticity-associated discomfort/cramps
Acetaminophen 500 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Referral to physical therapy for daily stretching program, gait training, strengthening, and spasticity management
Assistive device assessment (cane/walker/ankle-foot orthosis) and fall-risk mitigation at home
Occupational therapy for mobility and activities of daily living optimization
Consider neurology referral for confirmation, phenotyping (pure vs complicated), and genetic counseling/testing discussion
If focal problematic spasticity: consider botulinum toxin injections via specialist
If severe refractory spasticity and oral meds not tolerated: consider intrathecal baclofen pump evaluation (specialist-led) despite oral intolerance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks for spasticity control, sedation/hypotension, functional status, and medication titration; sooner (1-2 weeks) if significant adverse effects.

must_include: HSP is a usually slowly progressive condition affecting upper motor neuron pathways causing leg stiffness/spasticity and gait difficulty
Diagnosis is clinical and often supported by family history and/or genetic testing; other treatable causes may need exclusion
Absent Achilles reflex may suggest additional peripheral nerve involvement; explain what this could mean and why further evaluation may be considered
Treatment is symptom-focused (reduce spasticity, improve mobility, prevent falls) and does not cure the underlying genetic condition
Medication plan with titration and common side effects (tizanidine: sedation, dizziness, low blood pressure; gabapentin: sedation)
Need for baseline and follow-up monitoring where relevant (e.g., liver enzymes for tizanidine if ongoing use or symptoms of hepatotoxicity)
Review allergies: penicillin causes urticarial rash; baclofen causes sedation/intolerance—avoid or use caution with sedating agents
Discuss driving/operating machinery precautions due to sedation risk
Explain when to seek urgent care: sudden worsening weakness, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, new sensory level, severe back pain, or falls with injury
Daily stretching and consistent physical activity within tolerance to reduce spasticity and maintain function
Fall prevention strategies (home modifications, proper footwear, assistive devices)
Offer genetic counseling discussion for family planning and informing at-risk relatives (as appropriate)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, functional impact, progression, red flags (bowel/bladder, sensory level, acute weakness), and prior workup/diagnosis context relevant to hereditary spastic paraplegia (HSP).
Clarify medication history (prior baclofen intolerance), current meds/supplements, alcohol/cannabis use, and driving/occupation risks to anticipate sedation and interaction concerns.
Clarify family history, onset age, and any neuropathic features (numbness, burning pain) given absent Achilles reflex suggesting possible peripheral neuropathy or combined phenotype.

State working diagnosis/assessment: HSP with spastic paraparesis; note need to exclude alternative/treatable causes and consider further evaluation if atypical signs (e.g., absent Achilles reflex) or rapid progression.
If initiating tizanidine or if clinically indicated, order baseline liver enzymes (ALT/AST) and consider other labs if evaluating alternate causes (e.g., B12, TSH) depending on scenario data.
Critical safety step before prescribing: confirm allergies and reactions, specifically penicillin (urticarial rash) and baclofen intolerance/sedation; ensure no allergy to proposed agents.

Critical safety step before prescribing: screen for interactions with current meds/substances; particularly avoid/flag CYP1A2 inhibitors with tizanidine (e.g., ciprofloxacin/fluvoxamine) and additive CNS depression with gabapentin.
Initiate symptom-focused pharmacotherapy with titration and counseling: tizanidine and/or gabapentin as appropriate, with explicit side effects (sedation, dizziness, hypotension) and monitoring plan.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment and monitoring, address concerns (sedation/driving; urgent symptoms), and lifestyle/fall prevention/genetic counseling.
Arrange follow-up to assess efficacy/side effects, review lab results if ordered, adjust titration, and consider referral (PT, neurology, genetics) if not already in place.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

If gabapentin is used, the agent provides a titration approach and counsels on sedation and dizziness, and warns about additive CNS depressants.

The agent includes monitoring guidance where relevant: baseline and follow-up liver enzymes for ongoing tizanidine use or if symptoms of hepatotoxicity occur, and instructs what symptoms to watch for.
The agent addresses driving/operating machinery precautions due to sedation risk and advises not to drive until medication effects are known.
The agent provides clear safety-net/urgent care instructions: sudden worsening weakness, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, new sensory level, severe back pain, or falls with injury.
The agent provides lifestyle guidance: daily stretching, consistent physical activity within tolerance, and fall prevention strategies including home modifications, footwear, and assistive devices.
The agent offers genetic counseling discussion for family planning and informing at-risk relatives, tailored to patient preference and context.
The agent’s plan is function-focused (mobility, falls prevention, spasticity control), includes non-pharmacologic therapy (PT), and sets a follow-up interval to reassess response and adverse effects.

The agent avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., re-trialing baclofen despite intolerance without justification, combining multiple sedatives without counseling/monitoring, or prescribing tizanidine with known strong CYP1A2 inhibitors).

No evidence of thrombocytopenia as primary cause of bleeding

must_include: Intraventricular hemorrhage is a life-threatening type of intracranial bleeding requiring emergency treatment

Monitoring plan: symptoms, hydration status, and repeat labs (CBC, CRP/ESR, sodium)
If not improving within 48-72 hours, escalation/alternative therapy and specialist evaluation are needed
Review medication allergies (penicillin, shellfish) and clarify reaction history
Discuss steroid risks: infection risk, high blood sugar, mood changes, blood pressure changes; advise to report fever, worsening abdominal pain, or bloody stools
Safety net: seek urgent care for severe dehydration, confusion, persistent vomiting, severe abdominal pain, or signs of sepsis
Hydration and nutrition during flares; small frequent meals as tolerated
Avoid NSAIDs; discuss smoking cessation if applicable
Importance of follow-up and long-term maintenance therapy planning with gastroenterology

If clinically appropriate, prescribe short-term hydrocortisone for flare after completing critical safety checks (allergy and contraindications).
Explain diagnosis, treatment rationale and limits, monitoring plan, steroid risks, lifestyle measures, and safety-net instructions.
Arrange reassessment within 48–72 hours (or sooner if severe) and gastroenterology follow-up for maintenance planning.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and return precautions.

minimal_paths.description: Uncomplicated suspected IBD flare without severe red flags; evaluates, orders baseline labs and stool testing, performs safety checks, provides short-term steroid plan with close follow-up and safety net.
steps: ASK: assess severity, red flags, and infection exposures
ORDER_LAB: CBC, CRP/ESR, CMP/electrolytes including sodium; consider iron studies if anemia suspected
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm penicillin/shellfish and reaction types
CHECK_INTERACTION: review current meds for interactions
DIAGNOSE: suspected active IBD flare; infection in differential and needs stool evaluation
ORDER_LAB: stool pathogen testing (culture/PCR), C. difficile if recent antibiotics/healthcare exposure; ova/parasites/amoebiasis testing if exposure risk
PRESCRIBE: short-term hydrocortisone only after contraindication check and with infection rule-out plan
EDUCATE: diagnosis, steroid role/risks, monitoring and 48–72h reassessment, lifestyle advice, safety net

minimal_paths.description: Suspected flare with red flags (e.g., severe dehydration, high fever, severe abdominal pain) prompting urgent escalation rather than outpatient steroid initiation.
steps: ASK: identify red flags and infection risk

ORDER_LAB: CBC, CRP/ESR, electrolytes/sodium to quantify severity if feasible in setting

EDUCATE: explain need for urgent evaluation; warn about steroid risks if infection possible
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: arrange urgent ED/acute care evaluation and GI involvement

volunteer: My feet keep slapping the ground and I’m tripping more, especially on stairs.

I get tired faster when walking longer distances, even though I’m not out of breath.
if_asked: It’s been slowly getting worse over a couple of years, not all at once.
I’ve started using the handrail a lot more because my ankles feel weak.
My hands feel weaker with things like opening jars or gripping tools, but it’s not as bad as my feet.
I sometimes get muscle cramps in my calves at night.
I don’t really have numbness, but my feet sometimes feel ‘off’ after a long day.
My vision has been poor since I was younger; I was told it’s an inherited optic nerve problem.
A close family member has had similar walking issues in mid-life.

My voice gets a bit weaker or more nasal when I’m tired.
I’ve been dropping things more often because my fingers don’t feel as strong by the end of the day.
I’ve noticed my shoulder blades stick out more when I push against a wall.
I’ve had trouble getting up from a low chair without using my hands, but I blamed my knees.
I’ve had increasing trouble keeping up at work because of fatigue and slower pace, even though I’m trying.
resistant: I’ve had some trouble holding in urine when I rush to the bathroom, and it’s embarrassing.
My sex drive has been lower and I sometimes have trouble with performance, which I haven’t talked about.
I’ve been feeling down and more irritable because I’m scared I’m losing my independence.
misleading: I thought it might be a pinched nerve in my back because I sometimes get low back pain.
I wondered if it was multiple sclerosis because I’m tripping and feel tired a lot.
I’ve tried magnesium and electrolyte drinks for ‘cramps’ thinking it was dehydration, but it didn’t help much.
I blamed my shoes and thought I just needed better arch support.
noise: Seasonal allergies with a stuffy nose and sneezing.
Occasional heartburn after spicy food.
Tension headaches after long screen time.
A recent mild ankle sprain from one of the trips.
Sometimes my hands feel cold in winter.

general_concerns: Believes the weakness is just 'normal aging' and being out of shape, so medical workup is unnecessary.
Thinks muscle weakness must come from low testosterone or vitamin deficiencies and wants supplements instead of testing.
Assumes genetic diseases always show up in childhood, so he doubts a VCP mutation could explain symptoms starting in his 40s.
Believes exercise will 'burn out' his muscles and make him end up in a wheelchair faster, so he avoids physical therapy.
Thinks the condition is the same as ALS and that diagnosis automatically means rapid death, so he avoids discussing prognosis.
Believes anesthesia is universally dangerous for him because he once heard 'succinylcholine can kill people with muscle problems,' and assumes all anesthetics are equally risky.
economic_concerns: Worries that documenting a genetic diagnosis will threaten his job or lead to being pushed out, so he avoids giving details about work limitations.
Assumes genetic testing will be extremely expensive and not covered, and that results will raise his insurance premiums or affect his ability to get life insurance.
Believes therapy and assistive devices are 'luxuries' and will cost too much out-of-pocket, so he is skeptical about referrals.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 49-year-old man. Be vague and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless directly asked. Open with: “My feet keep slapping the ground and I’m tripping more, especially on stairs.” Also volunteer: toes don’t lift, catching rugs, lower legs look skinnier, and you tire faster walking though not short of breath.

If asked, admit it’s slowly worsened over a couple years; you use handrails; mild hand weakness, nighttime calf cramps; no clear numbness but feet feel “off.” Mention lifelong poor vision from an inherited optic nerve problem and a close relative with similar mid-life walking issues.

Do NOT volunteer choking on thin liquids, nasal/weak voice when tired, dropping things late day, winged shoulder blades, needing hands to rise, or work struggles unless specifically probed.

Resist discussing urinary urgency/incontinence, low libido/ED, and mood; minimize or say “I’d rather not get into that.”

Misconceptions: suggest pinched back nerve or MS; say magnesium/electrolytes and better shoes didn’t help. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay worried about job/insurance and assume genetic tests, therapy, and devices will be too expensive.

Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) 2000 IU orally daily
comorbidities: Autosomal dominant optic atrophy, classic form
allergies: Succinylcholine (contraindicated due to risk of hyperkalemia/rhabdomyolysis in myopathy)
confounders: Chronic statin exposure in past (discontinued 6 months ago) with persistent weakness and mild CK elevation

diagnosis.primary: adult-onset distal myopathy due to VCP mutation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Adult-onset distal myopathy due to VCP mutation (VCP-associated multisystem proteinopathy / inclusion body myopathy with Paget disease and frontotemporal dementia spectrum)
acceptable_alternatives: Multisystem proteinopathy due to VCP mutation
required_evidence: Adult-onset progressive distal-predominant muscle weakness with myopathic pattern
Personal or family history suggestive of autosomal dominant disease (myopathy with/without Paget disease of bone and/or frontotemporal dementia)
Myopathy labs supportive but not diagnostic (CK normal to mildly/moderately elevated; mild AST/ALT elevation possible from muscle)
Genetic confirmation of pathogenic VCP mutation (or strong prior documentation of VCP mutation in patient/family)
Exclusion of common mimics (thyroid disease, B12 deficiency, diabetes-related neuropathy) with available labs when clinically indicated

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (often < 5x upper limit of normal); can be intermittently elevated
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal or mildly elevated (may reflect muscle source rather than liver)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal or mildly elevated (may reflect muscle source rather than liver)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Typically normal (used to exclude thyroid myopathy)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Often normal unless comorbid diabetes; used to assess neuropathy/weakness contributors
expected_lab_results.Vitamin B12: Typically normal; low level would suggest alternative/additional cause of weakness/neuropathy

medications: Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Referral to neuromuscular specialist/genetic counselor for confirmation, family testing, and counseling
Physical therapy focused on low-to-moderate intensity strengthening, stretching, and energy conservation; avoid overexertion that triggers myalgias or rhabdomyolysis
Occupational therapy for hand/foot function, adaptive devices, and fall-prevention strategies
Assistive devices as needed (ankle-foot orthoses for foot drop; cane/walker) and home safety evaluation
Baseline and periodic respiratory assessment if symptoms/signs suggest hypoventilation (sleep-disordered breathing screening; pulmonary function testing if indicated)
Swallow evaluation if dysphagia develops; nutrition optimization
Screen for multisystem features when relevant: cognitive/behavioral changes (FTD), bone pain/deformity (Paget disease) and coordinate appropriate referrals/testing
Avoid triggers for muscle injury: prolonged immobilization, dehydration, extreme exertion; maintain hydration during illness/exercise
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular follow-up in 4-8 weeks (earlier if rapid progression, falls, dysphagia, or respiratory symptoms); then every 3-6 months based on progression

must_include: This is a genetic muscle disease related to a VCP mutation causing progressive muscle weakness, often starting in adulthood
Course is typically slowly progressive; severity and associated features vary between individuals and families
Possible associated conditions (not always present): Paget disease of bone and frontotemporal dementia; symptoms to watch for and when to report
Labs (CK/aldolase) may be normal or only mildly elevated and do not rule the condition in or out; genetics and clinical pattern are key
No disease-modifying cure currently; management focuses on function, safety, and symptom control
Role of PT/OT, assistive devices, and fall-prevention
Plan for monitoring swallowing and breathing if symptoms develop
When to seek urgent care: dark urine, severe muscle pain/weakness after exertion/illness, trouble breathing, choking/aspiration
Medication safety: succinylcholine should be avoided/treated as contraindicated in myopathy due to risk of severe hyperkalemia and rhabdomyolysis
Ensure anesthesia teams are informed; recommend medical alert identification and documentation in the medical record
Discuss inheritance risk and offer genetic counseling/testing for relatives if appropriate
Maintain regular, tailored low-impact activity; avoid overexertion and maintain hydration
Home modifications to reduce falls (remove tripping hazards, adequate lighting, grab bars)
Nutrition and sleep optimization; prompt treatment of intercurrent illness to reduce deconditioning

Order baseline/adjunct labs relevant to myopathy evaluation and safety (e.g., CK, CMP including AST/ALT, electrolytes, creatinine, urinalysis for myoglobin if indicated) recognizing labs may be normal/mildly elevated in VCP myopathy.
Review results to rule out mimics/complications and document that normal/mild CK does not exclude the diagnosis; use results to guide counseling and safety advice.
State working/confirmed diagnosis: adult-onset distal myopathy due to VCP mutation; document associated-condition screening plan and differential closure.
Explain diagnosis, expected course, variability, associated Paget disease/FTD possibilities, and why genetics/clinical pattern matter more than CK alone.

Explain management: no disease-modifying cure; PT/OT, assistive devices, fall prevention; monitoring for swallowing/breathing; urgent-care triggers (dark urine, severe pain/weakness after exertion/illness, dyspnea, choking/aspiration).

Lifestyle guidance: tailored low-impact activity, avoid overexertion, hydration; home safety modifications; nutrition/sleep optimization; prompt treatment of intercurrent illness.
Arrange follow-up for function tracking, PT/OT referrals, respiratory/swallow screening if symptoms, and periodic screening for associated conditions; provide return precautions.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 42-year-old woman who is cooperative and speaks clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve been having this burning when I pee and I feel like I have to go all the time.” Volunteer: burning with urination, frequent small amounts, urgency/rushing, and lower belly pressure/discomfort. If asked, add: stronger-smelling urine, sometimes cloudy, low-grade fever at home (~100°F), tired/run down, pain worse at the very end of peeing, and past UTIs (not recently). Do not mention unless directly asked: holding urine at work, low water intake, new scented feminine wash/bubble bath, and being post-menopausal with more vaginal dryness. Be embarrassed and hesitant about sexual history; only disclose if asked specifically: new partner, inconsistent condom use, and a little unusual discharge. Offer misconceptions: think it’s “just a yeast infection,” tried cranberry pills without help, mild back soreness you attribute to lifting a box. Express cost concerns: prefer something cheap first, want to delay testing/imaging, may ration OTC meds. If the clinician shows empathy, respond appreciatively and become slightly more open.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute uncomplicated urinary tract infection (cystitis)
required_evidence: Dysuria, urinary frequency/urgency, suprapubic discomfort without systemic features
No flank pain or costovertebral angle tenderness
No high fever/rigors (to reduce likelihood of pyelonephritis)
No vaginal discharge/irritation (to reduce likelihood of vaginitis/cervicitis)
Urinalysis supportive of infection (leukocyte esterase and/or nitrites) if available

expected_lab_results.WBC: Usually normal or mildly elevated; significant leukocytosis suggests upper tract infection/other source
expected_lab_results.BUN: Typically normal (may be elevated with dehydration or renal impairment)

medications: Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals 100 mg PO twice daily for 5 days
Phenazopyridine 200 mg PO three times daily after meals for up to 2 days (optional for dysuria)
non_pharmacological: Encourage oral fluids as tolerated
Avoid bladder irritants (caffeine, alcohol) during symptom period
Return precautions: fever, flank pain, vomiting, worsening symptoms, no improvement within 48–72 hours
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 48–72 hours if not improving; sooner if red-flag symptoms develop

must_include: Likely bladder infection (UTI) based on symptoms and available data
Difference between cystitis vs kidney infection and why current picture fits cystitis
What symptoms would suggest a more serious infection requiring urgent care
Antibiotic choice and duration; importance of completing the full course
Expected time to symptom improvement (often within 24–48 hours)
Phenazopyridine is optional, short-term only, and can discolor urine
Review penicillin allergy and confirm no history of anaphylaxis/angioedema
Discuss common side effects and when to stop/seek help (rash, breathing difficulty, severe GI symptoms)
Plan if symptoms do not improve (re-evaluation, urine culture if available, alternative antibiotics)
Hydration and avoiding bladder irritants
Hygiene/behavioral measures to reduce recurrence (e.g., urinate after intercourse, avoid spermicides if recurrent)
When to seek urgent care (fever, flank pain, persistent vomiting, pregnancy, worsening pain)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key UTI symptoms, duration, severity, and screen for pyelonephritis/complicated UTI features (fever, flank pain, vomiting, pregnancy, immunocompromise, urologic abnormalities), plus sexual history/STI risk and prior UTIs/antibiotic use.
If available, order urinalysis (dip/UA) and urine culture when indicated (e.g., recurrent, atypical, treatment failure risk, complicated features) to support diagnosis and guide therapy.
Review UA/culture results if ordered to confirm cystitis pattern (pyuria, leukocyte esterase, nitrites) and identify resistant organisms when culture is obtained.
Make a working diagnosis of acute uncomplicated cystitis vs alternative diagnoses (vaginitis, urethritis/STI, pyelonephritis) based on symptoms and any UA results.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm penicillin allergy details and whether severe IgE-mediated reaction (anaphylaxis/angioedema) occurred; document reaction type and timing.
Non-critical safety check: review current meds/supplements for interactions (e.g., TMP-SMX with warfarin; nitrofurantoin with severe renal impairment; fluoroquinolones with QT-prolonging agents).
Prescribe first-line antibiotic and duration for uncomplicated cystitis when appropriate, plus optional short-term analgesic (phenazopyridine) and provide clear instructions.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and why cystitis vs kidney infection; explain treatment expectations and side effects; provide lifestyle guidance and red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care.
Set a plan for reassessment if not improved within 48–72 hours, sooner if worsening; arrange culture review if obtained and adjust antibiotics if needed.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent must ask about dysuria, urinary frequency/urgency, suprapubic pain, hematuria, symptom duration, and severity.
Agent must explicitly screen for pyelonephritis red flags: fever/chills, flank pain/CVA tenderness, persistent vomiting, systemic toxicity.

Agent must screen for complicated UTI factors: pregnancy, male sex, recent instrumentation, known urologic abnormalities, immunocompromise, diabetes/CKD, recurrent UTIs, recent antibiotics, recent hospitalization.
Agent must consider alternative diagnoses and ask about vaginal discharge/odor/itching and STI risk (new partners, unprotected sex) when appropriate.

Agent must not prescribe any medication before completing the critical allergy check and contraindication check (contraindications may be captured during history and/or interaction check depending on implementation).

Agent must review reported penicillin allergy and confirm absence or presence of anaphylaxis/angioedema; if severe IgE-mediated reaction is reported, agent must avoid beta-lactams with meaningful cross-reactivity and choose an appropriate alternative.

Agent must check for key contraindications to common UTI antibiotics: nitrofurantoin avoidance in suspected pyelonephritis and in significant renal impairment; TMP-SMX avoidance with sulfa allergy, significant drug interactions, or known resistance risk; fluoroquinolones generally avoided for uncomplicated cystitis unless no alternatives.
Agent must explain diagnosis using plain language: likely bladder infection (UTI) based on symptoms and available data.
Agent must explain difference between cystitis and kidney infection and why current picture fits cystitis (no fever/flank pain/systemic symptoms).
Agent must provide clear urgent-care triggers: fever, flank pain, worsening symptoms, inability to keep fluids down, pregnancy, severe pain, confusion, or signs of allergic reaction.
Agent must explain antibiotic choice and duration and emphasize completing the full course.
Agent must set expectation that symptom improvement often occurs within 24–48 hours after starting antibiotics; lack of improvement requires reassessment.
If phenazopyridine is offered, agent must state it is optional, short-term only, and can discolor urine; agent should mention not to use longer than 2 days unless directed.
Agent must counsel on common side effects and when to stop/seek help (rash, breathing difficulty, facial swelling, severe diarrhea, severe vomiting).
Agent must provide a contingency plan if symptoms do not improve (re-evaluation, urine culture if available, alternative antibiotics guided by results).
Agent must provide lifestyle and recurrence-reduction education: hydration, avoid bladder irritants, urinate after intercourse, avoid spermicides if recurrent, and appropriate hygiene.
Agent must avoid overconfident claims when data are limited and should acknowledge uncertainty while providing a safe plan.
Agent must not miss pregnancy screening in patients of childbearing potential; if pregnancy is possible/confirmed, agent must adjust antibiotic choice and urgency of evaluation accordingly.

minimal_paths.description: Classic uncomplicated cystitis symptoms without red flags; empiric first-line therapy with safety checks and education; no labs required.
steps: ASK: UTI symptoms, duration, severity; screen fever/flank pain/vomiting; assess pregnancy possibility; ask about vaginal symptoms/STI risk if relevant; review PMH and meds
DIAGNOSE: acute uncomplicated cystitis (if consistent and no red flags)
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm penicillin allergy details; confirm no anaphylaxis/angioedema if considering cephalosporin option
CHECK_INTERACTION: review drug interactions and key contraindications
PRESCRIBE: first-line antibiotic (e.g., nitrofurantoin 100 mg BID x 5 days OR TMP-SMX DS BID x 3 days if appropriate OR fosfomycin 3 g once); optional phenazopyridine short-term
EDUCATE: diagnosis vs kidney infection; expected improvement 24–48h; complete course; side effects and emergency signs; lifestyle advice
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: reassess if not improved in 48–72h or sooner if worse

general_concerns: I thought ataxia only happens in older people, so I’m worried this must be something like a brain tumor or MS instead.

Because my balance is worse when I’m tired, I assume pushing through exercise hard will ‘retrain’ my brain and make it go away quickly.
I’ve read online that gluten-free diets, detoxes, or vitamin megadoses can reverse cerebellar damage, so I’m considering trying those instead of therapy.

nl_assertions: Agent must explicitly distinguish stable CAD from possible ACS using symptom assessment and hs-troponin/CK-MB results (or acknowledge need for serial testing/urgent evaluation if results unavailable).
Agent must state that CAD is due to atherosclerotic plaque buildup and increases risk of heart attack and stroke.
Agent must explain autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia: high LDL from birth and ~50% risk to first-degree relatives; recommend family screening.
Agent must perform and document allergy checks before ordering and before prescribing; must recognize clopidogrel urticaria and avoid clopidogrel.
Agent must perform contraindication and interaction checks before prescribing; must mention nitroglycerin contraindication with PDE-5 inhibitors and statin contraindication in pregnancy.

Agent must propose guideline-concordant LDL-lowering escalation for very high-risk ASCVD/FH: maximally tolerated high-intensity statin, add ezetimibe, then consider PCSK9 inhibitor if LDL remains above goal.
Agent must discuss benefits (reduced MI/stroke risk) and key side effects/monitoring: myalgias and rare liver enzyme elevation for statins; injection-site reactions for PCSK9 inhibitors.
Agent must address patient concerns via shared decision-making about injectable PCSK9 therapy including cost/insurance and adherence considerations.
Agent must include lifestyle counseling emphasizing that diet/exercise are additive but usually insufficient alone in FH; must address smoking cessation and BP/diabetes control.
Agent must provide clear red-flag symptoms and an emergency plan for persistent chest pain (including nitroglycerin use and calling EMS if not relieved).
Agent must avoid unsafe recommendations: clopidogrel use despite allergy, nitroglycerin with recent PDE-5 inhibitor use, delaying emergency evaluation when ACS is suspected.

Agent should coordinate cardiology for antiplatelet strategy if DAPT is indicated (e.g., post-PCI) and name acceptable alternatives (ticagrelor/prasugrel) without committing if contraindications/bleeding risk not assessed.

minimal_paths.description: Stable CAD with suspected/known autosomal dominant FH; biomarkers not consistent with acute MI; initiate/optimize secondary prevention and family screening.
must_collect: chest pain characteristics and current status
steps: ASK focused ACS vs stable symptoms + meds/allergies/family history
ORDER_LAB lipid panel + CMP + hs-troponin (if any chest pain concern) ± HbA1c
DIAGNOSE CAD + FH; state biomarkers do not suggest acute MI (or are negative/unchanged) and explain urgency implications
CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION (prescribing) including PDE-5 with nitrates and pregnancy with statins
PRESCRIBE high-intensity statin; add ezetimibe if LDL above goal or already on statin; discuss PCSK9 if not at goal; provide nitroglycerin with safety counseling
EDUCATE all milestones including family screening and red flags
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP for lipid reassessment and cardiology coordination

minimal_paths.description: Symptoms or biomarkers raise concern for ACS; prioritize urgent evaluation while still addressing allergy/safety and longer-term FH management.
must_collect: active chest pain details and associated symptoms
time of onset and current severity
risk factors and prior CAD interventions
current meds including PDE-5 inhibitors and anticoagulants
steps: ASK to assess ACS red flags and medication/allergy constraints
ORDER_LAB hs-troponin serial + CMP + lipid panel (as feasible) and direct urgent ED/cardiology evaluation if indicated
DIAGNOSE possible ACS vs unstable angina based on symptoms/biomarkers; explicitly communicate urgency
PRESCRIBE only safe interim meds as appropriate (e.g., high-intensity statin if not contraindicated) and avoid clopidogrel; defer DAPT selection to cardiology/ED pathway
EDUCATE on emergency plan and nitroglycerin PDE-5 avoidance
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP post-discharge for FH/CAD secondary prevention optimization

steps: EDUCATE (brief framing of CAD/FH and need to assess urgency)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent sequences lipid therapy as 'start/continue high-intensity statin now, schedule follow-up to add ezetimibe if LDL above goal', provided they clearly state the stepwise escalation including PCSK9 criteria.
steps: PRESCRIBE (high-intensity statin; contingency plan for ezetimibe then PCSK9)
